frames.caption: Grow a sorted prefix left-to-right. The first element [5] is a sorted prefix of length 1 on its own.
frames.note: Insertion sort builds the answer one element at a time, keeping the left side always sorted. It is O(n²) worst case but O(n) on already-sorted data and very fast on small or nearly-sorted arrays — which is why production sorts (like Timsort) switch to it for tiny subarrays.

frames.caption: Take key = a[1] = 2. The prefix a[0..0] is already sorted; slide the key left into place.
frames.note: Take the first element of the unsorted region as the "key". Everything to its left is already sorted, so the only question is where the key belongs within that prefix. We find its spot by sliding it left past anything larger — exactly like inserting a card into a sorted hand.

frames.caption: a[0] = 5 > key = 2: the larger element shifts right and the key moves left to index 0.
frames.note: The key is smaller than the element on its left, so that larger element shifts up one slot to make room. Using a strict > comparison (not ≥) is what keeps the sort STABLE — equal keys never jump over each other, so their original order survives. Each slide removes exactly one inversion, which is why the total cost is O(n + inversions).

frames.caption: a[start] ≤ key, so 2 settles at index 0. Sorted prefix is now a[0..1].
frames.note: The key has met an element ≤ itself (or the start of the array), so it drops into the gap and the sorted prefix grows by one. Because we only ever shifted strictly-larger elements, equal values kept their order and no element was compared more than necessary — the reason insertion sort is O(n) on already-sorted input.

frames.caption: Every element has been inserted into the sorted prefix — the array is sorted.
frames.note: Insertion sort finished in place (O(1) extra space) and stably. Its adaptivity — cheap when data is already close to sorted — is exactly why it is the go-to for the small base cases inside larger divide-and-conquer sorts.
